title: Savings Bonds in 2026: Are They Still Worth It? (EE vs I Bonds Explained)
description: Rates are changing find out if savings bonds still beat inflation in 2026. Compare EE vs I bonds, current returns, and who should invest now.
alt: Stack of U.S. savings bonds with a calculator and calendar

content.text: Disclaimer: This content is for educational purposes only and does not constitute financial advice. Always consult a qualified financial advisor before making investment decisions.

If you've ever wondered whether savings bonds still make sense in 2026, you're not alone. With high‑yield savings accounts paying around 4% and inflation still on everyone's mind, savings bonds look more interesting than they have in years.
But are they the right fit for your portfolio? Here's a straight‑forward, no‑hype breakdown of how savings bonds work, what they pay right now, and who they're actually for.
What Are U.S. Savings Bonds?
When you buy a U.S. savings bond, you're lending money to the U.S. government. In return, the government promises to pay you back with interest.

items: Simple – Buy once and earn interest for up to 30 years.
Safe – Backed by the full faith and credit of the U.S. government.
Affordable – You can start with as little as $25.

content.text: Savings bonds are low‑risk, government‑backed assets designed for individual investors, not for trading on the open market. You buy them directly from the Treasury, hold them for a period of time, and cash them when you need the money.
Two Types of Savings Bonds: EE vs. I

Series EE Bonds – The Fixed‑Rate Option
Current rate: 2.50% (for bonds issued November 2025 through April 2026)
How it works: The rate is fixed for the first 20 years.
The doubling guarantee: At 20 years, the bond will be worth at least double its purchase price, regardless of interest rates.
EE bonds are predictable. You know exactly what you're getting. However, the current 2.50% rate is quite low compared to many other safe options right now.

Series I Bonds – The Inflation‑Protected Option
Current composite rate: 4.03% (for bonds issued November 2025 through April 2026)
How it works: A fixed rate (currently 0.90%) plus a variable rate that adjusts every six months based on inflation.
Current inflation component: 3.12%
I bonds are designed to protect your purchasing power. When inflation goes up, so does your return. That's why they paid over 9% in 2022 when inflation spiked.
For I bonds purchased from May through October 2026, the projected rate will be approximately 4.26%–4.36% (3.36% variable + 0.90% fixed).
Key distinction: I bonds protect against inflation; EE bonds do not. If you're worried about rising prices, I bonds are worth a closer look.
The Good and the Not‑So‑Good
Pros of Savings Bonds

rows: You cannot lose principal. The U.S. government guarantees repayment.
Your return rises with inflation. No other safe asset offers this automatically.
Interest is exempt from state and local income tax. Federal tax is deferred until you cash the bond.
If you use the bonds for qualified higher education expenses, you may exclude the interest from federal income tax, subject to income limits.
There are no commissions, management fees, or account minimums beyond the $25 purchase minimum.

content.text: Cons of Savings Bonds

rows: Low returns relative to other options
2.50%–4.03% may not beat long‑term stock market averages (historically 7–10%).
You cannot cash a savings bond for the first 12 months. This is emergency‑fund money only after year one.
Cash out within the first five years, and you lose the last three months of interest.
You can only buy $10,000 per series per year ($20,000 total for EE and I bonds).
Savings bonds are not tradeable. You can only redeem them directly with the Treasury.
Bonds stop earning interest after three decades. If you hold them past maturity, you lose potential earnings.

content.text: Who Should Buy Savings Bonds?
Savings bonds might be a good fit if:

items: You want safety above all else.
You're saving for a long‑term goal (10+ years) and want inflation protection without stock market risk.
You're in a high‑tax state.
You're saving for a child's college education.
You've already maxed out other safe options like a high‑yield savings account or CD.

content.text: Savings bonds are probably not for you if:

items: You need access to your money within the next 12 months.
You're seeking high returns.
You want to invest large amounts (above $10,000 per year).
You don't want to deal with TreasuryDirect.

content.text: How Savings Bonds Compare to Other Safe Options (April 2026)

rows: Locked for term, penalty for early withdrawal

content.text: The bottom line: I bonds are competitive with high-yield savings accounts and CDs, with the added benefit of inflation protection and state tax exemption. However, the one‑year lock‑up period and five‑year penalty window make them less flexible.
Tax Treatment: What You Need to Know
One of the most underrated features of savings bonds is their tax treatment.

items: Federal tax: You can defer reporting interest until you redeem the bond or it stops earning interest after 30 years.
State and local tax: Interest is completely exempt.

Education exclusion: If you redeem qualified Series EE or I bonds and use the proceeds to pay for qualified higher education expenses, the interest may be completely tax‑free. For 2025, this benefit phases out for single filers with MAGI between $99,500 and $114,500, and for joint filers between $149,250 and $179,250.

content.text: Important: To qualify for the education exclusion, the bond owner must be at least 24 years old at the time of purchase. Bonds bought in a child's name do not qualify.
How to Buy Savings Bonds
You can only buy electronic savings bonds through TreasuryDirect.gov.

items: 1. Create an account at TreasuryDirect.gov.
2. Link your bank account.
3. Choose Series EE or Series I.
4. Enter the purchase amount ($25 minimum; $10,000 maximum per series per year).
5. Confirm and buy.

content.text: Once purchased, the bonds appear in your online account. You can hold them there for up to 30 years.
The Bottom Line
Savings bonds are not get rich quick tools. They are boring, safe, and predictable and that's exactly why they have a place in certain portfolios.
Buy I bonds if: You want safety and inflation protection, can leave the money untouched for at least one year (ideally five), or live in a high‑tax state.
Consider EE bonds only if: You are certain you can hold them for 20 years to lock in the doubling guarantee, or you prefer a completely fixed rate with no surprises.
Skip savings bonds if: You need immediate liquidity, are chasing higher returns, or want to invest more than $10,000 per year in this asset class.

For most beginners building wealth from scratch, your priority should still be: emergency fund → high-interest debt → low-cost index funds in a tax advantaged retirement account. Savings bonds can be a nice addition after those boxes are checked – especially for money you want to keep absolutely safe while beating inflation.

If you're just starting out with little or no money, the most important step isn't picking the right bond or stock – it's having a clear, repeatable system. That's exactly why I wrote my complete building wealth from scratch guide. It walks you through the four‑step framework (earn → save → invest → grow) that works even when you're broke. Once you have that foundation, then you can decide where tools like savings bonds fit into your bigger picture.

final_advice.text: The best investment is the one you understand and can stick with. Savings bonds won't make you rich, but they can keep you safe. Use them as a tool, not a lottery ticket.

faq.q: Can I lose money with savings bonds?
faq.a: No. As long as you hold them for at least one year, you will get back at least your original investment plus interest earned (minus any penalty if cashed within five years).

faq.q: Are savings bonds better than a high-yield savings account?
faq.a: It depends. If you need immediate access to your money, a HYSA is better. If you want inflation protection and state tax exemption, I bonds may win. Right now, the rates are similar, so liquidity is the main trade‑off.

faq.q: Should I buy I bonds or EE bonds?
faq.a: For most people in 2026, I bonds make more sense. They pay significantly more (4.03% vs. 2.50%) and offer inflation protection. EE bonds are only appealing if you are certain you can hold them for 20 years to get the doubling guarantee, which works out to an effective annual return of about 3.5%.

faq.q: What happens if I need to cash a savings bond early?
faq.a: You must hold the bond for at least 12 months. If you cash it between years 1 and 5, you lose the last three months of interest. After five years, there is no penalty.

faq.q: Are savings bonds worth it for retirement?
faq.a: Generally no. For long‑term retirement savings, stocks and stock‑based index funds have historically provided much higher returns. Savings bonds are better for shorter‑term goals, emergency funds (after year one), or the conservative portion of your portfolio.

faq.q: Can I buy savings bonds for my child?
faq.a: Yes. You can purchase bonds as a gift or open a minor linked account through TreasuryDirect.
